metadata.title: Square the integral, spin it into polar coordinates
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL=1D bell curve / x-direction; CRIMSON=2D surface / r-direction; GOLD=polar grid / rotation highlight; SLATE=axes/structure
metadata.note: Self-contained math gem. ROTATE: Cartesian to polar. Show squaring trick then polar substitution. Result = sqrt(pi). Underlies every QM normalization. 10-beat.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

skin_warnings: B01: palette=claude but the cold open is 'un-annotated' — COLD OPEN LAW wants ClaudeComposerAsk
B12: palette=claude but the outro is 'OutroCTA' — OUTRO LAW wants ClaudeTitleOutro

beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. You can't integrate e to the minus x squared the normal way. So square it and rotate.
beats.visual:
CARD: eyebrow QUANTUM MECHANICS, headline 'You can't integrate
e^(-x²) directly.
Square it. Rotate.'

build.suggested: suggested prompt: This is Liam, in for Bear. You can't integrate e to the minus x squared the normal way. So square it and rotate.

beats.narration_text: The Gaussian integral — the integral from minus infinity to infinity of e to the minus x squared — has no elementary antiderivative. Yet it appears in every quantum wavefunction normalization. How do you actually compute it?
beats.visual:
CARD: eyebrow THE QUESTION, headline 'How do you compute
∫e^{-x²}dx
when it has no antiderivative?'

build.suggested: suggested prompt: The Gaussian integral — the integral from minus infinity to infinity of e to the minus x squared — has no elementary antiderivative. Yet it appears in every qua

beats.narration_text: The integral I equals the integral from minus infinity to infinity of e to the minus x squared d x. Call it I. Plot the bell curve: it's symmetric, positive, decaying to zero. The area under it looks finite — but no substitution, no integration by parts, gives you a closed form.
beats.visual: Axes with Gaussian bell curve (TEAL). Label I = integral e^{-x^2} dx. Show area under curve shaded TEAL. Note: 'no elementary antiderivative'.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: The integral I equals the integral from minus infinity to infinity of e to the minus x squared d x. Call it I. Plot the bell curve: it's symmetric, positive, de

beats.narration_text: The trick: compute I squared. I squared equals the integral of e to the minus x squared d x, times the integral of e to the minus y squared d y. Since the two integrals are independent — just different names for the same variable — this is a double integral over the entire plane: the integral of e to the minus x squared plus y squared, d x d y.
beats.visual: Show I^2 = (∫e^{-x^2}dx)(∫e^{-y^2}dy) = ∫∫ e^{-(x^2+y^2)} dx dy. TEAL text, then 2D bell surface illustration.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: The trick: compute I squared. I squared equals the integral of e to the minus x squared d x, times the integral of e to the minus y squared d y. Since the two i

beats.narration_text: The exponent x squared plus y squared is the distance from the origin squared — it's r squared in polar coordinates. Switch to polar: x equals r cosine theta, y equals r sine theta, and the area element d x d y becomes r d r d theta. That r is the key.
beats.visual: Left: Cartesian grid with x^2+y^2 = r^2 circle. Right: polar grid with r and theta labeled. Arrow showing coordinate change. GOLD polar grid, SLATE Cartesian.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: The exponent x squared plus y squared is the distance from the origin squared — it's r squared in polar coordinates. Switch to polar: x equals r cosine theta, y

beats.narration_text: In polar coordinates, I squared becomes: the integral from 0 to 2 pi d theta, times the integral from 0 to infinity of e to the minus r squared times r d r. The theta integral gives 2 pi. The r integral is now elementary: substitute u equals r squared, and it gives one half. So I squared equals 2 pi times one half equals pi. Therefore I equals root pi.
beats.visual: Step-by-step: polar form = 2pi * integral_0^inf e^{-r^2} r dr. Substitute u=r^2: = 2pi * (1/2) = pi. So I = sqrt(pi). CRIMSON for each step, TEAL for final result.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: In polar coordinates, I squared becomes: the integral from 0 to 2 pi d theta, times the integral from 0 to infinity of e to the minus r squared times r d r. The

beats.narration_text: This result underlies all of quantum mechanics. The normalized Gaussian wavefunction is e to the minus x squared over 4 sigma squared, divided by a factor of the fourth root of 2 pi sigma squared. That normalization constant comes directly from the Gaussian integral.
beats.visual: QM Gaussian psi(x) = (2*pi*sigma^2)^{-1/4} * exp(-x^2/4sigma^2). Show the normalization integral = 1 and how sqrt(pi) appears. TEAL wavefunction, CRIMSON normalization integral.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: This result underlies all of quantum mechanics. The normalized Gaussian wavefunction is e to the minus x squared over 4 sigma squared, divided by a factor of th

beats.narration_text: The same trick evaluates every Gaussian moment. The expectation value of x squared is the integral of x squared times the Gaussian. Integrate by parts, or differentiate the Gaussian integral with respect to a parameter — every such integral reduces back to root pi with a rational prefactor.
beats.visual: Show <x^2> = integral x^2 * (Gaussian) dx. Connect to the same trick: differentiate I(a) = integral e^{-ax^2} dx = sqrt(pi/a) with respect to a. TEAL for the parameter trick.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: The same trick evaluates every Gaussian moment. The expectation value of x squared is the integral of x squared times the Gaussian. Integrate by parts, or diffe

beats.narration_text: The normalized Gaussian: integral from minus infinity to infinity of e to the minus x squared d x equals root pi. Therefore the normalized probability density one over root pi times e to the minus x squared integrates to exactly 1. The probability of finding the particle somewhere is certain.
beats.visual: Clean equation: (1/sqrt(pi)) * integral_{-inf}^{inf} e^{-x^2} dx = (1/sqrt(pi)) * sqrt(pi) = 1. TEAL for (1/sqrt(pi)), CRIMSON for integral = sqrt(pi), GOLD for = 1. Below: normalization confirmed bar.

beats.narration_text: Square the integral. Switch to polar. The r in d-r kills the exponential. Out drops root pi.
beats.visual:
CARD: eyebrow QUANTUM MECHANICS, headline 'Square it: I² = ∫∫e^{-r²}dA.
Switch to polar: r dr dθ.
I² = π  →  I = √π.'

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Square the integral. Switch to polar. The r in d-r kills the exponential. Out drops root pi.
